chunk_summary: Rapid breastfeeding assessment and escalation for poor weight gain in infants under 6 months focuses on structured evaluation, safety screening, feeding history, maternal risks, and tiered interventions to protect breastfeeding while addressing weight concerns.
learning_objectives: Understand the 90-second safety screen criteria for urgent referral in breastfeeding infants.
Learn how to conduct a focused feeding history and identify maternal risks affecting milk supply.
Recognize red flags for low milk transfer and implement tiered escalation strategies.
Apply techniques to protect breastfeeding while addressing poor weight gain.
key_facts: Infants should have 8–12 effective feeds in 24 hours during early infancy.
Failure to regain birth weight by 10–14 days or continued weight loss requires escalation.
Urgent referral is needed for lethargy, fever, hypothermia, dehydration, or respiratory distress during feeds.
Tier 1 intervention includes correcting latch, increasing effective feeds, and adding breast compression.
Tier 2 involves adding expressed mother’s milk and pumping at the frequency of replaced feeds.
reference_quote: Failure to regain birth weight by 10–14 days, or continued weight loss, requires escalation. (Source: ABM clinical protocol 3 supplementary feedings in the healthy term breastfed neonate)
document_key_topics: Rapid breastfeeding assessment for poor weight gain
Safety screening and urgent referral criteria
Tiered escalation strategies to protect breastfeeding
